range.nativeRange: Central and southern Europe, western Asia

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US — introduced ornamental

invasive.description: Not listed as invasive in any US state; self-seeds moderately but does not spread aggressively or displace native vegetation; safe for cultivation across the lower 48

description.timeToMaturity: Blooms from first or second season
description.lifespan: Perennial (unlike D. purpurea which is biennial); 5-10 years; self-sows to maintain colony
description.deciduousEvergreen: Semi-evergreen — basal rosette of leaves often persists through mild winters; dies back in hard winters
description.winterInterest: Low to moderate — basal rosette present in mild winters; clean and tidy

lightAndWater.sun: Part shade to full shade — one of the very few yellow-flowering plants that performs well in shade; this is its primary design value
lightAndWater.water: Medium; drought tolerant in shade once established

claySoilPerformance.description: Tolerates clay well; may perform better with organic matter incorporated at planting to improve structure and drainage; avoid waterlogged conditions which can cause crown rot

soil.texturePreference: Adaptable; tolerates clay and poor soils
soil.rootCharacter: Fibrous taproot; does not spread vegetatively; colony maintained by self-seeding

bloom.bloomTime: June to July; may rebloom if cut back after first flush
bloom.bloomDescription: Tubular, pendulous, creamy-yellow flowers to 2" long with brown interior markings, borne in terminal racemes to 12" long atop upright leafy stems
bloom.pollination: Primarily bumblebees (the tubular flowers are sized for large bees); also attracts hummingbirds

yield.medicinalUses: ALL Digitalis species are the botanical source of digoxin and related cardiac glycosides; these compounds are used in pharmaceutical medicine for heart conditions but are ONLY safe under strict medical supervision; do not attempt any home medicinal use

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: HIGHLY TOXIC — all parts, but especially leaves; contains digitoxin, digoxin, and related cardiac glycosides; ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, irregular heartbeat, and can be fatal; even skin contact with sap can cause rash in sensitive individuals. Assess child and public access to any planting site before installing.
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: HIGHLY TOXIC to dogs, cats, and horses — cardiac glycoside effects; potentially fatal; fallen leaves and seeds are a risk as animals may investigate novel plants. Consider placement carefully relative to pet access.

permaculture.coppiceable: Cut flowering stems to the base after bloom; this often prompts a second flush of flowers later in summer

wildlife.attracts: Bumblebees (the primary and most enthusiastic visitors — the tubular flower is a near-perfect fit), hummingbirds; minimal other wildlife value

deer.description: Excellent — among the most reliably deer-resistant plants available; extremely toxic and strongly avoided by deer across all US regions

drought.description: Moderate in shade; will struggle in hot, dry, sunny conditions

tolerances.other: Tolerates shade, clay, and poor soil well; cold-hardy to Zone 3; heat performance declines in Zone 8 and above without consistent shade and moisture

maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut flowering stems to the basal rosette immediately after bloom to encourage rebloom and prevent excessive self-seeding. Basal rosette overwinters; remove winter-damaged leaves in early spring. Allow some seedheads to mature to maintain the self-seeding colony — mark parent plants so you don't accidentally weed out seedlings.
propagationMethods: Seed (self-sows moderately; direct sow in fall or cold-stratify for spring; germinates readily)
division (possible in spring but not typically necessary)

cultivarNotes:
The straight species is the most widely available and most ornamental in this group, performing reliably across Zones 3-8. 'Carillon' is a compact selection at 18-24" — suited to beds with height restrictions. No other cultivars are widely grown across the US market. Note: do not confuse with Digitalis lutea (small yellow foxglove), which is similar but smaller (18-24") and also a strong choice where reduced height is desirable.
In the warmer end of the range (Zones 7-8), plant in deeper shade and ensure consistent moisture through summer; the species struggles with heat stress in full sun. In Zones 3-5, the basal rosette may not overwinter above ground but the plant re-emerges reliably from roots; mulch after hard frost to protect crown.

gardenUse.designPlacement: Mid-zone position in a shade or part-shade border where yellow is needed in the palette — a role very few shade-tolerant plants can fill. Excellent with blue and purple companions (Geranium, Ajuga, Astilbe) to create contrast. Always assess child and pet access given high toxicity before finalizing placement.
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus:
To source
Pennsylvania — Hegins Site Observations:

pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus: Not listed by the PA Department of Agriculture or PINVASIVES
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Red clay in Hegins is compacted and slow-draining after rain; amend planting hole with compost and ensure site does not pond; north-facing placement in Section A provides adequate moisture retention without waterlogging in typical conditions
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Red clay at Hegins site; tolerates without amendment but will establish faster with compost worked in; avoid heavy clay that stays saturated
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: The straight species and 'Carillon' are both well-matched to Zone 6a conditions. At Hegins, the north-facing exposure of Section A keeps temperatures moderate and prevents summer heat stress — ideal conditions for this species. 'Carillon' is preferred for Section A given the 5' height limit.
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Section A north-facing front bed, mid-zone — provides the yellow note in the purple/pink/yellow palette from a shade-tolerant species; no other candidate in the Section A plant list offers this combination. Toxicity warrants confirmation that child and pet access to the front bed is limited or supervised; if access is regular and unsupervised, consider relocating to Section B where access is more controlled.
